content.problemStatement:
Coding in Sprout (a programming language) is very intuitive. Chef is giving his minions a demonstration in Sprout and wants you to
help him determine if they are not too difficult for them.



A program in Sprout is written using three kinds of instructions.




Load Instruction: Load a value into buffer.
	Increment Instruction: Increment the value in buffer.
	Print Instruction: Print the value from buffer.

The buffer stores a single integer between 0 and 25, both inclusive. If the value in the buffer is x, the increment instruction
makes the value (x+1), if x < 25. At x = 25 the increment instruction makes the value 0.



Load Instruction can be used to load any value between 0 and 25 (inclusive) into the buffer.



Print from the buffer prints a lowercase English character based on the value in the buffer. Precisely, it prints the (x+1)th character
in the alphabet. Thus, for x = 0, 'a' is printed; x = 1, 'b' is printed and so on. For x = 25, 'z' is printed.



To keep his programs clear, he uses the load instruction only once in the beginning (before printing any character). Then
he proceeds instruction after instruction. A program is simple, if the number of instructions is not more than ELEVEN times the length
of the word that it prints.
Given the word Chef wants his program to print and assuming he will write the shortest code (i.e. use the fewest instructions)
to print it, will the program be simple?
constraints: All characters are lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single word consisting of lowercase English letters (a‑z).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line containing "YES" if the shortest possible program that prints the given word uses at most 11 × |word| instructions, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: For "helloworld" (length 10) the minimal instruction count is 1 (load) + 10 (prints) + 100 (increments) = 111, which exceeds 11*10=110, so output is NO. For "mississippi" (length 11) the count is 1+11+100=112 ≤ 11*11=121, so output is YES.

solution.approach:
The program can use exactly one Load instruction at the beginning. After loading the value that corresponds to the first character, a Print instruction is needed for every character. Between two consecutive characters we may only use Increment instructions, which move the buffer forward cyclically (0→1→…→25→0). For two consecutive characters c_prev and c_cur the minimal number of increments is (value(c_cur) - value(c_prev) + 26) % 26. Therefore the optimal number of instructions is:
    total = 1               // the initial Load
          + |word|          // one Print for each character
          + Σ diff_i        // diff_i is the forward distance between consecutive characters.
If total <= 11 * |word| the program is considered simple.
The algorithm scans the word once, computes the sum of forward distances and checks the inequality. Time complexity is O(|word|) per test case, and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N) where N is the length of each word (≤1000).

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        s = next(it)
        n = len(s)
        total = 1 + n  # load + prints
        for i in range(1, n):
            diff = ord(s[i]) - ord(s[i-1])
            if diff < 0:
                diff += 26
            total += diff
        out.append('YES' if total <= 11 * n else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('
'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
